Employee Turnover¶

Problem Scenario¶

Portobello Tech is an app innovator who has devised an intelligent way of predicting employee turnover within the company. It periodically evaluates employees' work details, including the number of projects they worked on, average monthly working hours, time spent in the company, promotions in the last five years, and salary level.

Data from prior evaluations shows the employees’ satisfaction in the workplace. The data could be used to identify patterns in work style and their interest in continuing to work for the company.

The HR Department owns the data and uses it to predict employee turnover. Employee turnover refers to the total number of workers who leave a company over time.

As the ML Developer assigned to the HR Department, you have been asked to create ML programs to:

  • Perform data quality checks by checking for missing values, if any.
  • Understand what factors contributed most to employee turnover at EDA.
  • Perform clustering of employees who left based on their satisfaction and evaluation.
  • Handle the left Class Imbalance using the SMOTE technique.
  • Perform k-fold cross-validation model training and evaluate performance.
  • Identify the best model and justify the evaluation metrics used.
  • Suggest various retention strategies for targeted employees.

Employee Data¶

Column Name Description
satisfaction_level Satisfaction level at the job of an employee
last_evaluation Rating between 0 and 1, received by an employee at his last evaluation
number_project The number of projects an employee is involved in
average_montly_hours Average number of hours in a month spent by an employee at the office
time_spend_company Number of years spent in the company
Work_accident 0 - no accident during employee stay, 1 - accident during employee stay
left 0 indicates an employee stays with the company and 1 indicates an employee left the company
promotion_last_5years Number of promotions in his stay
Department Department to which an employee belongs to
salary Salary in USD

Data: HR_common_sep.csv

Perform the following steps:¶

  • Perform data quality checks by checking for missing values, if any.

  • Understand what factors contributed most to employee turnover at EDA.

    • Draw a heatmap of the correlation matrix between all numerical features or columns in the data.
    • Draw the distribution plot of:
      • Employee Satisfaction (use column satisfaction_level)
      • Employee Evaluation (use column last_evaluation)
      • Employee Average Monthly Hours (use column average_montly_hours)
      • Draw the bar plot of the employee project count of both employees who left and stayed in the organization (use column number_project and hue column left), and give your inferences from the plot.

  • Perform clustering of employees who left based on their satisfaction and evaluation.

    • Choose columns satisfaction_level, last_evaluation, and left.
    • Do K-means clustering of employees who left the company into 3 clusters?
    • Based on the satisfaction and evaluation factors, give your thoughts on the employee clusters.

  • Handle the left Class Imbalance using the SMOTE technique.

    • Pre-process the data by converting categorical columns to numerical columns by:
    • Separating categorical variables and numeric variables
    • Applying get_dummies() to the categorical variables
    • Combining categorical variables and numeric variables
    • Do the stratified split of the dataset to train and test in the ratio 80:20 with random_state=123.
    • Upsample the train dataset using the SMOTE technique from the imblearn module.

  • Perform 5-fold cross-validation model training and evaluate performance.

    • Train a logistic regression model, apply a 5-fold CV, and plot the classification report.
    • Train a Random Forest Classifier model, apply the 5-fold CV, and plot the classification report.
    • Train a Gradient Boosting Classifier model, apply the 5-fold CV, and plot the classification report.

  • Identify the best model and justify the evaluation metrics used.

    • Find the ROC/AUC for each model and plot the ROC curve.
    • Find the confusion matrix for each of the models.
    • Explain which metric needs to be used from the confusion matrix: Recall or Precision?

  • Suggest various retention strategies for targeted employees.

    • Using the best model, predict the probability of employee turnover in the test data.
    • Based on the probability score range below, categorize the employees into four zones and suggest your thoughts on the retention strategies for each zone.
    • Safe Zone (Green) (Score < 20%)
    • Low-Risk Zone (Yellow) (20% < Score < 60%)
    • Medium-Risk Zone (Orange) (60% < Score < 90%)
    • High-Risk Zone (Red) (Score > 90%).
In [1]:
print("hello world")

hello world

1. Import Libraries and Load Data¶

In [1]:
# Import necessary libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Set visualization style
sns.set_style('whitegrid')
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (12, 6)

# Display settings
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 100)
In [2]:
# Load the dataset
df = pd.read_csv('HR_comma_sep.csv')

# Display basic information about the dataset
print("Dataset Shape:", df.shape)
print("\n" + "="*50)
print("First Few Rows:")
print(df.head())
print("\n" + "="*50)
print("Dataset Info:")
print(df.info())
print("\n" + "="*50)
print("Statistical Summary:")
print(df.describe())

2. Data Quality Checks¶

In [3]:
# Check for missing values
print("Missing Values Check:")
print("="*50)
missing_values = df.isnull().sum()
print(missing_values)
print("\n" + "="*50)
print(f"Total Missing Values: {missing_values.sum()}")
print(f"Percentage of Missing Values: {(missing_values.sum() / len(df)) * 100:.2f}%")

# Check for duplicates
duplicates = df.duplicated().sum()
print("\n" + "="*50)
print(f"Number of Duplicate Rows: {duplicates}")

# Check data types
print("\n" + "="*50)
print("Data Types:")
print(df.dtypes)

3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

3.1 Correlation Heatmap¶

In [4]:
# Create correlation matrix for numerical features
numerical_features = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()
correlation_matrix = df[numerical_features].corr()

# Plot heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm',
            center=0, square=True, linewidths=1, cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.8})
plt.title('Correlation Matrix of Numerical Features', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Key insights from correlation
print("\nKey Correlations with 'left' (Employee Turnover):")
print(correlation_matrix['left'].sort_values(ascending=False))

Key Correlations with 'left' (Employee Turnover):
left                     1.000000
time_spend_company       0.144822
average_montly_hours     0.071287
number_project           0.023787
last_evaluation          0.006567
promotion_last_5years   -0.061788
Work_accident           -0.154622
satisfaction_level      -0.388375
Name: left, dtype: float64

3.2 Distribution Plots¶

In [5]:
# Create distribution plots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(16, 12))

# Employee Satisfaction Distribution
sns.histplot(data=df, x='satisfaction_level', hue='left', kde=True, ax=axes[0, 0], bins=30)
axes[0, 0].set_title('Distribution of Employee Satisfaction Level', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('Satisfaction Level')
axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[0, 0].legend(['Stayed', 'Left'])

# Employee Evaluation Distribution
sns.histplot(data=df, x='last_evaluation', hue='left', kde=True, ax=axes[0, 1], bins=30)
axes[0, 1].set_title('Distribution of Employee Last Evaluation', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('Last Evaluation Score')
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[0, 1].legend(['Stayed', 'Left'])

# Average Monthly Hours Distribution
sns.histplot(data=df, x='average_montly_hours', hue='left', kde=True, ax=axes[1, 0], bins=30)
axes[1, 0].set_title('Distribution of Average Monthly Hours', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('Average Monthly Hours')
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[1, 0].legend(['Stayed', 'Left'])

# Project Count Bar Plot
project_left = df.groupby(['number_project', 'left']).size().unstack()
project_left.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1, 1], color=['#2ecc71', '#e74c3c'])
axes[1, 1].set_title('Employee Project Count by Turnover Status', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1, 1].set_xlabel('Number of Projects')
axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[1, 1].legend(['Stayed', 'Left'])
axes[1, 1].set_xticklabels(axes[1, 1].get_xticklabels(), rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Generate insights from the data
print("="*70)

# Turnover rate

print(f"\n1. Overall Turnover Rate: {turnover_rate:.2f}%")

# Satisfaction analysis

print(f"   - Average satisfaction (Stayed): {df[df['left']==0]['satisfaction_level'].mean():.3f}")
print(f"   - Average satisfaction (Left): {df[df['left']==1]['satisfaction_level'].mean():.3f}")

# Evaluation analysis
print("\n3. Last Evaluation Analysis:")
print(f"   - Average evaluation (Stayed): {df[df['left']==0]['last_evaluation'].mean():.3f}")
print(f"   - Average evaluation (Left): {df[df['left']==1]['last_evaluation'].mean():.3f}")

# Project count analysis
print("\n4. Project Count Analysis:")
print(df.groupby('left')['number_project'].value_counts().unstack(fill_value=0))

# Hours analysis
print("\n5. Average Monthly Hours:")
print(f"   - Average hours (Stayed): {df[df['left']==0]['average_montly_hours'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"   - Average hours (Left): {df[df['left']==1]['average_montly_hours'].mean():.1f}")

print("\n" + "="*70)

4. K-Means Clustering Analysis¶

In [7]:
# Import clustering libraries
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Filter employees who left
left_employees = df[df['left'] == 1][['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation', 'left']].copy()

print(f"Number of employees who left: {len(left_employees)}")

# Prepare features for clustering
X_cluster = left_employees[['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation']]

# Standardize features
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_cluster)

# Perform K-Means clustering with 3 clusters
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
left_employees['cluster'] = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# Analyze clusters
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("CLUSTER ANALYSIS")
print("="*70)
print("\nCluster Centers (Scaled):")
print(pd.DataFrame(kmeans.cluster_centers_,
                   columns=['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation'],
                   index=['Cluster 0', 'Cluster 1', 'Cluster 2']))

print("\nCluster Statistics (Original Scale):")
cluster_stats = left_employees.groupby('cluster')[['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation']].agg(['mean', 'std', 'count'])
print(cluster_stats)

Number of employees who left: 3571

======================================================================
CLUSTER ANALYSIS
======================================================================

Cluster Centers (Scaled):
           satisfaction_level  last_evaluation
Cluster 0           -0.105323        -1.016202
Cluster 1            1.394233         1.003320
Cluster 2           -1.245858         0.772956

Cluster Statistics (Original Scale):
        satisfaction_level                 last_evaluation                
                      mean       std count            mean       std count
cluster                                                                   
0                 0.412304  0.066943  1667        0.517265  0.047611  1667
1                 0.808031  0.077337   965        0.916415  0.069475   965
2                 0.111321  0.046548   939        0.870884  0.064958   939
In [8]:
# Visualize clusters
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))

# Scatter plot with clusters
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
scatter = plt.scatter(left_employees['satisfaction_level'],
                     left_employees['last_evaluation'],
                     c=left_employees['cluster'],
                     cmap='viridis',
                     s=50,
                     alpha=0.6,
                     edgecolors='black')
plt.xlabel('Satisfaction Level', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Last Evaluation', fontsize=12)
plt.title('K-Means Clustering of Employees Who Left', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.colorbar(scatter, label='Cluster')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Cluster distribution
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
cluster_counts = left_employees['cluster'].value_counts().sort_index()
bars = plt.bar(cluster_counts.index, cluster_counts.values, color=['#3498db', '#e74c3c', '#2ecc71'])
plt.xlabel('Cluster', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Number of Employees', fontsize=12)
plt.title('Distribution of Employees Across Clusters', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xticks([0, 1, 2])
for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()
    plt.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2., height,
             f'{int(height)}',
             ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [9]:
# Cluster interpretation
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("CLUSTER INTERPRETATION")
print("="*70)

for cluster_id in range(3):
    cluster_data = left_employees[left_employees['cluster'] == cluster_id]
    avg_satisfaction = cluster_data['satisfaction_level'].mean()
    avg_evaluation = cluster_data['last_evaluation'].mean()

    print(f"\nCluster {cluster_id}: ({len(cluster_data)} employees)")
    print(f"  - Average Satisfaction: {avg_satisfaction:.3f}")
    print(f"  - Average Evaluation: {avg_evaluation:.3f}")

    # Interpretation
    if avg_satisfaction < 0.5 and avg_evaluation < 0.6:
        print("  - Profile: Underperformers (Low satisfaction, Low evaluation)")
        print("  - Likely reason: Poor fit or disengagement")
    elif avg_satisfaction < 0.5 and avg_evaluation >= 0.6:
        print("  - Profile: Overworked (Low satisfaction, High evaluation)")
        print("  - Likely reason: Burnout, work-life balance issues")
    else:
        print("  - Profile: High performers seeking growth")
        print("  - Likely reason: Limited growth opportunities, better offers elsewhere")

======================================================================
CLUSTER INTERPRETATION
======================================================================

Cluster 0: (1667 employees)
  - Average Satisfaction: 0.412
  - Average Evaluation: 0.517
  - Profile: Underperformers (Low satisfaction, Low evaluation)
  - Likely reason: Poor fit or disengagement

Cluster 1: (965 employees)
  - Average Satisfaction: 0.808
  - Average Evaluation: 0.916
  - Profile: High performers seeking growth
  - Likely reason: Limited growth opportunities, better offers elsewhere

Cluster 2: (939 employees)
  - Average Satisfaction: 0.111
  - Average Evaluation: 0.871
  - Profile: Overworked (Low satisfaction, High evaluation)
  - Likely reason: Burnout, work-life balance issues

5. Data Preprocessing and SMOTE¶

In [10]:
# Import preprocessing libraries
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE

# Separate features and target
X = df.drop('left', axis=1)
y = df['left']

# Separate categorical and numerical variables
categorical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns.tolist()
numerical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()

print("Categorical Columns:", categorical_cols)
print("Numerical Columns:", numerical_cols)

# Apply one-hot encoding to categorical variables
X_encoded = pd.get_dummies(X, columns=categorical_cols, drop_first=True)

print(f"\nOriginal feature count: {X.shape[1]}")
print(f"Encoded feature count: {X_encoded.shape[1]}")
print(f"\nEncoded columns: {list(X_encoded.columns)}")

Categorical Columns: ['sales', 'salary']
Numerical Columns: ['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation', 'number_project', 'average_montly_hours', 'time_spend_company', 'Work_accident', 'promotion_last_5years']

Original feature count: 9
Encoded feature count: 18

Encoded columns: ['satisfaction_level', 'last_evaluation', 'number_project', 'average_montly_hours', 'time_spend_company', 'Work_accident', 'promotion_last_5years', 'sales_RandD', 'sales_accounting', 'sales_hr', 'sales_management', 'sales_marketing', 'sales_product_mng', 'sales_sales', 'sales_support', 'sales_technical', 'salary_low', 'salary_medium']
In [11]:
# Stratified train-test split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_encoded, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=123, stratify=y
)

print("="*70)
print("TRAIN-TEST SPLIT")
print("="*70)
print(f"Training set size: {X_train.shape[0]} ({(X_train.shape[0]/len(df))*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Test set size: {X_test.shape[0]} ({(X_test.shape[0]/len(df))*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"\nClass distribution in training set:")
print(y_train.value_counts())
print(f"\nClass distribution in test set:")
print(y_test.value_counts())

======================================================================
TRAIN-TEST SPLIT
======================================================================
Training set size: 11999 (80.0%)
Test set size: 3000 (20.0%)

Class distribution in training set:
left
0    9142
1    2857
Name: count, dtype: int64

Class distribution in test set:
left
0    2286
1     714
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [12]:
# Apply SMOTE to handle class imbalance
smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_train_balanced, y_train_balanced = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

print("\n" + "="*70)
print("SMOTE APPLICATION")
print("="*70)
print(f"Original training set size: {X_train.shape[0]}")
print(f"Balanced training set size: {X_train_balanced.shape[0]}")
print(f"\nClass distribution before SMOTE:")
print(y_train.value_counts())
print(f"\nClass distribution after SMOTE:")
print(y_train_balanced.value_counts())

# Visualize class balance
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Before SMOTE
y_train.value_counts().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[0], color=['#2ecc71', '#e74c3c'])
axes[0].set_title('Class Distribution Before SMOTE', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Class (0=Stayed, 1=Left)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[0].set_xticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'], rotation=0)

# After SMOTE
y_train_balanced.value_counts().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1], color=['#2ecc71', '#e74c3c'])
axes[1].set_title('Class Distribution After SMOTE', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Class (0=Stayed, 1=Left)')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[1].set_xticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'], rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

======================================================================
SMOTE APPLICATION
======================================================================
Original training set size: 11999
Balanced training set size: 18284

Class distribution before SMOTE:
left
0    9142
1    2857
Name: count, dtype: int64

Class distribution after SMOTE:
left
0    9142
1    9142
Name: count, dtype: int64

6. Model Training with 5-Fold Cross-Validation¶

In [13]:
# Import machine learning libraries
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, cross_val_predict
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_auc_score, roc_curve

# Standardize features for Logistic Regression
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train_balanced)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

6.1 Logistic Regression¶

In [14]:
# Train Logistic Regression
print("="*70)
print("LOGISTIC REGRESSION MODEL")
print("="*70)

lr_model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)

# 5-fold cross-validation scores
cv_scores_lr = cross_val_score(lr_model, X_train_scaled, y_train_balanced, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
print(f"\n5-Fold CV Accuracy Scores: {cv_scores_lr}")
print(f"Mean CV Accuracy: {cv_scores_lr.mean():.4f} (+/- {cv_scores_lr.std():.4f})")

# Train on full training set
lr_model.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train_balanced)

# Predictions
y_pred_lr = lr_model.predict(X_test_scaled)
y_pred_proba_lr = lr_model.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]

# Classification Report
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_lr, target_names=['Stayed', 'Left']))

======================================================================
LOGISTIC REGRESSION MODEL
======================================================================

5-Fold CV Accuracy Scores: [0.73913043 0.78014766 0.80639869 0.82417282 0.82330416]
Mean CV Accuracy: 0.7946 (+/- 0.0320)

Classification Report:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

      Stayed       0.91      0.78      0.84      2286
        Left       0.51      0.74      0.60       714

    accuracy                           0.77      3000
   macro avg       0.71      0.76      0.72      3000
weighted avg       0.81      0.77      0.78      3000

6.2 Random Forest Classifier¶

In [15]:
# Train Random Forest
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("RANDOM FOREST CLASSIFIER")
print("="*70)

rf_model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)

# 5-fold cross-validation scores
cv_scores_rf = cross_val_score(rf_model, X_train_balanced, y_train_balanced, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
print(f"\n5-Fold CV Accuracy Scores: {cv_scores_rf}")
print(f"Mean CV Accuracy: {cv_scores_rf.mean():.4f} (+/- {cv_scores_rf.std():.4f})")

# Train on full training set
rf_model.fit(X_train_balanced, y_train_balanced)

# Predictions
y_pred_rf = rf_model.predict(X_test)
y_pred_proba_rf = rf_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Classification Report
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_rf, target_names=['Stayed', 'Left']))

# Feature importance
feature_importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X_encoded.columns,
    'importance': rf_model.feature_importances_
}).sort_values('importance', ascending=False)

print("\nTop 10 Important Features:")
print(feature_importance.head(10))

      Stayed       0.99      0.99      0.99      2286
        Left       0.97      0.98      0.97       714

6.3 Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

In [16]:
# Train Gradient Boosting
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("GRADIENT BOOSTING CLASSIFIER")
print("="*70)

gb_model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

# 5-fold cross-validation scores
cv_scores_gb = cross_val_score(gb_model, X_train_balanced, y_train_balanced, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
print(f"\n5-Fold CV Accuracy Scores: {cv_scores_gb}")
print(f"Mean CV Accuracy: {cv_scores_gb.mean():.4f} (+/- {cv_scores_gb.std():.4f})")

# Train on full training set
gb_model.fit(X_train_balanced, y_train_balanced)

# Predictions
y_pred_gb = gb_model.predict(X_test)
y_pred_proba_gb = gb_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Classification Report
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_gb, target_names=['Stayed', 'Left']))

Classification Report:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

      Stayed       0.98      0.98      0.98      2286
        Left       0.92      0.94      0.93       714

    accuracy                           0.97      3000
   macro avg       0.95      0.96      0.95      3000
weighted avg       0.97      0.97      0.97      3000

7. Model Evaluation and Comparison¶

7.1 ROC Curves and AUC Scores¶

In [17]:
# Calculate ROC AUC scores
roc_auc_lr = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba_lr)
roc_auc_rf = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba_rf)
roc_auc_gb = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba_gb)

# Calculate ROC curves
fpr_lr, tpr_lr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_pred_proba_lr)
fpr_rf, tpr_rf, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_pred_proba_rf)
fpr_gb, tpr_gb, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_pred_proba_gb)

# Plot ROC curves
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.plot(fpr_lr, tpr_lr, label=f'Logistic Regression (AUC = {roc_auc_lr:.4f})', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(fpr_rf, tpr_rf, label=f'Random Forest (AUC = {roc_auc_rf:.4f})', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(fpr_gb, tpr_gb, label=f'Gradient Boosting (AUC = {roc_auc_gb:.4f})', linewidth=2)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', label='Random Classifier', linewidth=2)

plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate', fontsize=12)
plt.title('ROC Curves for All Models', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("="*70)
print("ROC AUC SCORES")
print("="*70)
print(f"Logistic Regression: {roc_auc_lr:.4f}")
print(f"Random Forest: {roc_auc_rf:.4f}")
print(f"Gradient Boosting: {roc_auc_gb:.4f}")

======================================================================
ROC AUC SCORES
======================================================================
Logistic Regression: 0.8160
Random Forest: 0.9955
Gradient Boosting: 0.9863

7.2 Confusion Matrices¶

In [ ]:
# Calculate confusion matrices
cm_lr = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_lr)
cm_rf = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_rf)
cm_gb = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_gb)

# Plot confusion matrices
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

# Logistic Regression
sns.heatmap(cm_lr, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', ax=axes[0], cbar=False)
axes[0].set_title('Logistic Regression\nConfusion Matrix', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Predicted')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Actual')
axes[0].set_xticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])
axes[0].set_yticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])

# Random Forest
sns.heatmap(cm_rf, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Greens', ax=axes[1], cbar=False)
axes[1].set_title('Random Forest\nConfusion Matrix', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Predicted')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Actual')
axes[1].set_xticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])
axes[1].set_yticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])

# Gradient Boosting
sns.heatmap(cm_gb, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Oranges', ax=axes[2], cbar=False)
axes[2].set_title('Gradient Boosting\nConfusion Matrix', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[2].set_xlabel('Predicted')
axes[2].set_ylabel('Actual')
axes[2].set_xticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])
axes[2].set_yticklabels(['Stayed', 'Left'])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7.3 Metric Analysis and Best Model Selection¶

In [18]:
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, accuracy_score

# Calculate metrics for all models
models = {
    'Logistic Regression': (y_pred_lr, y_pred_proba_lr, roc_auc_lr),
    'Random Forest': (y_pred_rf, y_pred_proba_rf, roc_auc_rf),
    'Gradient Boosting': (y_pred_gb, y_pred_proba_gb, roc_auc_gb)
}

results = []
for model_name, (y_pred, y_pred_proba, auc) in models.items():
    accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    precision = precision_score(y_test, y_pred)
    recall = recall_score(y_test, y_pred)
    f1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred)

    results.append({
        'Model': model_name,
        'Accuracy': accuracy,
        'Precision': precision,
        'Recall': recall,
        'F1-Score': f1,
        'ROC-AUC': auc
    })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
print("="*70)
print("MODEL COMPARISON")
print("="*70)
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

# Visualize comparison
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

# Bar plot of metrics
results_df.set_index('Model')[['Accuracy', 'Precision', 'Recall', 'F1-Score', 'ROC-AUC']].plot(
    kind='bar', ax=axes[0], width=0.8
)
axes[0].set_title('Model Performance Comparison', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Score', fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Model', fontsize=12)
axes[0].legend(loc='lower right')
axes[0].set_xticklabels(axes[0].get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha='right')
axes[0].set_ylim([0, 1.05])
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y')

# Focus on Recall and Precision
results_df[['Model', 'Precision', 'Recall']].set_index('Model').plot(
    kind='bar', ax=axes[1], color=['#3498db', '#e74c3c'], width=0.7
)
axes[1].set_title('Precision vs Recall', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Score', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Model', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(axes[1].get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha='right')
axes[1].set_ylim([0, 1.05])
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

======================================================================
MODEL COMPARISON
======================================================================
              Model  Accuracy  Precision   Recall  F1-Score  ROC-AUC
Logistic Regression  0.768000   0.508637 0.742297  0.603645 0.816001
      Random Forest  0.988000   0.972145 0.977591  0.974860 0.995463
  Gradient Boosting  0.966667   0.924033 0.936975  0.930459 0.986321
In [19]:
# Metric selection explanation
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("METRIC SELECTION: RECALL vs PRECISION")
print("="*70)
print("""
In the context of Employee Turnover Prediction:

RECALL (Sensitivity) is MORE IMPORTANT because:
- False Negatives (missing employees who will leave) are costly
- We want to identify ALL employees at risk of leaving
- It's better to flag someone who might leave (and provide retention efforts)
  than to miss someone who actually leaves
- Cost of losing an employee: recruitment, training, productivity loss

PRECISION is less critical because:
- False Positives (incorrectly flagging employees as leaving) are less costly
- Extra retention efforts on stable employees may still boost morale
- The cost is primarily in HR time/resources, not business-critical losses

RECOMMENDATION: Use RECALL as the primary metric for model selection.
However, maintain reasonable precision to avoid resource waste.

BEST MODEL SELECTION:
""")

# Identify best model based on recall
best_model_idx = results_df['Recall'].idxmax()
best_model_name = results_df.loc[best_model_idx, 'Model']
best_recall = results_df.loc[best_model_idx, 'Recall']

print(f"Best Model: {best_model_name}")
print(f"Recall Score: {best_recall:.4f}")
print(f"ROC-AUC Score: {results_df.loc[best_model_idx, 'ROC-AUC']:.4f}")
print(f"F1-Score: {results_df.loc[best_model_idx, 'F1-Score']:.4f}")

======================================================================
METRIC SELECTION: RECALL vs PRECISION
======================================================================

In the context of Employee Turnover Prediction:

RECALL (Sensitivity) is MORE IMPORTANT because:
- False Negatives (missing employees who will leave) are costly
- We want to identify ALL employees at risk of leaving
- It's better to flag someone who might leave (and provide retention efforts)
  than to miss someone who actually leaves
- Cost of losing an employee: recruitment, training, productivity loss

PRECISION is less critical because:
- False Positives (incorrectly flagging employees as leaving) are less costly
- Extra retention efforts on stable employees may still boost morale
- The cost is primarily in HR time/resources, not business-critical losses

RECOMMENDATION: Use RECALL as the primary metric for model selection.
However, maintain reasonable precision to avoid resource waste.

BEST MODEL SELECTION:

Best Model: Random Forest
Recall Score: 0.9776
ROC-AUC Score: 0.9955
F1-Score: 0.9749

8. Retention Strategies Based on Risk Zones¶

In [20]:
# Use the best model for predictions (assuming Random Forest based on typical performance)
# Select the best model
if best_model_name == 'Logistic Regression':
    best_model = lr_model
    y_pred_proba_best = y_pred_proba_lr
elif best_model_name == 'Random Forest':
    best_model = rf_model
    y_pred_proba_best = y_pred_proba_rf
else:
    best_model = gb_model
    y_pred_proba_best = y_pred_proba_gb

# Create risk categories
test_results = pd.DataFrame({
    'actual': y_test.values,
    'probability': y_pred_proba_best * 100  # Convert to percentage
})

# Define risk zones
def categorize_risk(prob):
    if prob < 20:
        return 'Safe Zone (Green)'
    elif prob < 60:
        return 'Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)'
    elif prob < 90:
        return 'Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)'
    else:
        return 'High-Risk Zone (Red)'

test_results['risk_zone'] = test_results['probability'].apply(categorize_risk)

# Risk zone distribution
print("="*70)
print("EMPLOYEE RISK ZONE DISTRIBUTION")
print("="*70)
print(test_results['risk_zone'].value_counts().sort_index())
print(f"\nTotal employees in test set: {len(test_results)}")

======================================================================
EMPLOYEE RISK ZONE DISTRIBUTION
======================================================================
risk_zone
High-Risk Zone (Red)          661
Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)        112
Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)      44
Safe Zone (Green)            2183
Name: count, dtype: int64

Total employees in test set: 3000
In [21]:
# Visualize risk zones
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

# Risk zone distribution
risk_colors = {'Safe Zone (Green)': '#2ecc71',
               'Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)': '#f1c40f',
               'Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)': '#e67e22',
               'High-Risk Zone (Red)': '#e74c3c'}

risk_counts = test_results['risk_zone'].value_counts()
risk_order = ['Safe Zone (Green)', 'Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)',
              'Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)', 'High-Risk Zone (Red)']
risk_counts = risk_counts.reindex(risk_order)

bars = axes[0].bar(range(len(risk_counts)), risk_counts.values,
                    color=[risk_colors[zone] for zone in risk_order])
axes[0].set_xticks(range(len(risk_order)))
axes[0].set_xticklabels(risk_order, rotation=45, ha='right')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Employees', fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Employees Across Risk Zones', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y')

# Add value labels on bars
for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()
    axes[0].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2., height,
                f'{int(height)}',
                ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

# Probability distribution histogram
axes[1].hist(test_results['probability'], bins=20, edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7)
axes[1].axvline(20, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Safe Threshold (20%)')
axes[1].axvline(60, color='orange', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Medium Risk (60%)')
axes[1].axvline(90, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='High Risk (90%)')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Turnover Probability (%)', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_ylabel('Frequency', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_title('Distribution of Turnover Probabilities', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].legend()
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [22]:
# Retention strategies for each zone
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("RETENTION STRATEGIES BY RISK ZONE")
print("="*70)

strategies = {
    'Safe Zone (Green)': {
        'Risk Level': 'Very Low (< 20%)',
        'Description': 'Satisfied and engaged employees unlikely to leave',
        'Strategies': [
            '✓ Maintain current engagement practices',
            '✓ Regular check-ins to ensure continued satisfaction',
            '✓ Recognize and reward good performance',
            '✓ Provide growth opportunities to prevent future risk',
            '✓ Focus resources on higher-risk employees'
        ]
    },
    'Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)': {
        'Risk Level': 'Low (20% - 60%)',
        'Description': 'Generally satisfied but may have minor concerns',
        'Strategies': [
            '✓ Conduct stay interviews to understand concerns',
            '✓ Provide clear career development paths',
            '✓ Ensure competitive compensation and benefits',
            '✓ Address work-life balance concerns',
            '✓ Increase recognition and appreciation',
            '✓ Monitor for changes in engagement levels'
        ]
    },
    'Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)': {
        'Risk Level': 'Medium (60% - 90%)',
        'Description': 'Showing signs of disengagement, requires immediate attention',
        'Strategies': [
            '✓ URGENT: Schedule one-on-one meetings with managers',
            '✓ Identify and address specific pain points',
            '✓ Consider role adjustments or transfers',
            '✓ Review and adjust workload if necessary',
            '✓ Offer professional development opportunities',
            '✓ Provide mentorship or coaching',
            '✓ Re-evaluate compensation package',
            '✓ Create personalized retention plan'
        ]
    },
    'High-Risk Zone (Red)': {
        'Risk Level': 'Very High (> 90%)',
        'Description': 'Critical risk of leaving, immediate intervention required',
        'Strategies': [
            '✓ CRITICAL: Immediate intervention by senior management',
            '✓ Emergency meeting to discuss concerns and solutions',
            '✓ Assess if retention is feasible and beneficial',
            '✓ Consider counter-offers if employee is valuable',
            '✓ Offer significant role changes or promotions',
            '✓ Provide retention bonuses or incentives',
            '✓ Address any toxic work environment issues',
            '✓ If departure is inevitable, plan for smooth transition',
            '✓ Conduct exit interview to learn and improve',
            '✓ Begin succession planning immediately'
        ]
    }
}

for zone, info in strategies.items():
    print(f"\n{zone}")
    print("-" * 70)
    print(f"Risk Level: {info['Risk Level']}")
    print(f"Description: {info['Description']}")
    print(f"\nRecommended Strategies:")
    for strategy in info['Strategies']:
        print(f"  {strategy}")
    print()

======================================================================
RETENTION STRATEGIES BY RISK ZONE
======================================================================

Safe Zone (Green)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Risk Level: Very Low (< 20%)
Description: Satisfied and engaged employees unlikely to leave

Recommended Strategies:
  ✓ Maintain current engagement practices
  ✓ Regular check-ins to ensure continued satisfaction
  ✓ Recognize and reward good performance
  ✓ Provide growth opportunities to prevent future risk
  ✓ Focus resources on higher-risk employees


Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Risk Level: Low (20% - 60%)
Description: Generally satisfied but may have minor concerns

Recommended Strategies:
  ✓ Conduct stay interviews to understand concerns
  ✓ Provide clear career development paths
  ✓ Ensure competitive compensation and benefits
  ✓ Address work-life balance concerns
  ✓ Increase recognition and appreciation
  ✓ Monitor for changes in engagement levels


Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Risk Level: Medium (60% - 90%)
Description: Showing signs of disengagement, requires immediate attention

Recommended Strategies:
  ✓ URGENT: Schedule one-on-one meetings with managers
  ✓ Identify and address specific pain points
  ✓ Consider role adjustments or transfers
  ✓ Review and adjust workload if necessary
  ✓ Offer professional development opportunities
  ✓ Provide mentorship or coaching
  ✓ Re-evaluate compensation package
  ✓ Create personalized retention plan


High-Risk Zone (Red)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Risk Level: Very High (> 90%)
Description: Critical risk of leaving, immediate intervention required

Recommended Strategies:
  ✓ CRITICAL: Immediate intervention by senior management
  ✓ Emergency meeting to discuss concerns and solutions
  ✓ Assess if retention is feasible and beneficial
  ✓ Consider counter-offers if employee is valuable
  ✓ Offer significant role changes or promotions
  ✓ Provide retention bonuses or incentives
  ✓ Address any toxic work environment issues
  ✓ If departure is inevitable, plan for smooth transition
  ✓ Conduct exit interview to learn and improve
  ✓ Begin succession planning immediately
In [23]:
# Statistical analysis of risk zones
print("="*70)
print("RISK ZONE STATISTICS")
print("="*70)

for zone in risk_order:
    zone_data = test_results[test_results['risk_zone'] == zone]
    actual_turnover = zone_data['actual'].sum()
    total_in_zone = len(zone_data)
    turnover_rate = (actual_turnover / total_in_zone * 100) if total_in_zone > 0 else 0
    avg_probability = zone_data['probability'].mean()

    print(f"\n{zone}:")
    print(f"  - Total employees: {total_in_zone}")
    print(f"  - Actual turnover: {actual_turnover} ({turnover_rate:.1f}%)")
    print(f"  - Average predicted probability: {avg_probability:.1f}%")

======================================================================
RISK ZONE STATISTICS
======================================================================

Safe Zone (Green):
  - Total employees: 2183
  - Actual turnover: 11 (0.5%)
  - Average predicted probability: 2.8%

Low-Risk Zone (Yellow):
  - Total employees: 112
  - Actual turnover: 6 (5.4%)
  - Average predicted probability: 33.0%

Medium-Risk Zone (Orange):
  - Total employees: 44
  - Actual turnover: 40 (90.9%)
  - Average predicted probability: 75.4%

High-Risk Zone (Red):
  - Total employees: 661
  - Actual turnover: 657 (99.4%)
  - Average predicted probability: 99.2%
In [24]:
# Summary and recommendations
print("\n" + "="*70)
print("EXECUTIVE SUMMARY AND KEY RECOMMENDATIONS")
print("="*70)
print(f"""
1. MODEL PERFORMANCE:
   - Best Model: {best_model_name}
   - Recall Score: {best_recall:.2%} (successfully identifies {best_recall:.2%} of at-risk employees)
   - ROC-AUC Score: {results_df.loc[best_model_idx, 'ROC-AUC']:.4f}

2. RISK DISTRIBUTION:
   - High Risk: {len(test_results[test_results['risk_zone']=='High-Risk Zone (Red)'])} employees need immediate attention
   - Medium Risk: {len(test_results[test_results['risk_zone']=='Medium-Risk Zone (Orange)'])} employees require proactive intervention
   - Low Risk: {len(test_results[test_results['risk_zone']=='Low-Risk Zone (Yellow)'])} employees need monitoring
   - Safe: {len(test_results[test_results['risk_zone']=='Safe Zone (Green)'])} employees are stable

3. KEY ACTIONS:
   ✓ Prioritize High-Risk employees for immediate retention efforts
   ✓ Develop personalized retention plans for Medium-Risk employees
   ✓ Implement regular engagement surveys for Low-Risk employees
   ✓ Maintain current practices for Safe Zone employees

4. RESOURCE ALLOCATION:
   - Allocate 50% of retention budget to High-Risk zone
   - Allocate 30% to Medium-Risk zone
   - Allocate 15% to Low-Risk zone
   - Allocate 5% to Safe zone (preventive measures)

5. MONITORING:
   - Re-assess employee risk scores quarterly
   - Track effectiveness of retention strategies
   - Update model with new data annually
   - Conduct exit interviews for all departures to improve predictions
""")

======================================================================
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY AND KEY RECOMMENDATIONS
======================================================================

1. MODEL PERFORMANCE:
   - Best Model: Random Forest
   - Recall Score: 97.76% (successfully identifies 97.76% of at-risk employees)
   - ROC-AUC Score: 0.9955

2. RISK DISTRIBUTION:
   - High Risk: 661 employees need immediate attention
   - Medium Risk: 44 employees require proactive intervention
   - Low Risk: 112 employees need monitoring
   - Safe: 2183 employees are stable

3. KEY ACTIONS:
   ✓ Prioritize High-Risk employees for immediate retention efforts
   ✓ Develop personalized retention plans for Medium-Risk employees
   ✓ Implement regular engagement surveys for Low-Risk employees
   ✓ Maintain current practices for Safe Zone employees

4. RESOURCE ALLOCATION:
   - Allocate 50% of retention budget to High-Risk zone
   - Allocate 30% to Medium-Risk zone
   - Allocate 15% to Low-Risk zone
   - Allocate 5% to Safe zone (preventive measures)

5. MONITORING:
   - Re-assess employee risk scores quarterly
   - Track effectiveness of retention strategies
   - Update model with new data annually
   - Conduct exit interviews for all departures to improve predictions
